ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for hazelnut milk as of 2026; SNOMED CT coverage of plant-based milk alternatives is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard glass of hazelnut milk is approximately 240 ml (240 g), equivalent to a typical single-serve portion as consumed in Singapore and globally.

nutrient_source.title: Hazelnut milk (packaged) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged hazelnut milk (cr_id F01020008). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, vitamins, and minerals not reported in the HPB record are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of plant-based milk alternatives — analogous estimate based on oat milk and almond milk GI data; hazelnut milk GI estimated at 39 (low) consistent with nut-based plant milks
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for packaged hazelnut milk was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. GI is estimated at 39 (low) based on analogous nut-based plant milks (almond milk GI ~25–30; oat milk GI ~69; soy milk GI ~34) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Hazelnut milk, being nut-based with moderate sugar content (5.83 g/100 g), is estimated conservatively at GI 39. Glycemic load per 240 g serving = (39 × 9.45 × 2.40) / 100 = 8.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (5.83 g/100 g) in a sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk; sodium is moderate at 49 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should choose unsweetened hazelnut milk variants (0 g added sugar) as the added sugar in this sweetened packaged product independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance, despite the relatively low sodium content of 49 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 5.83 g sugar/100 g and negligible dietary fibre (0.41 g/100 g); liquid sugar absorbed rapidly
type2_diabetes.guidance: This sweetened packaged hazelnut milk is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its added sugar content (5.83 g/100 g) and negligible fibre (0.41 g/100 g), which provides no meaningful attenuation of glucose absorption; unsweetened variants with zero added sugar should be selected instead.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar content elevates CVD risk; fat content is low (0.72 g/100 g) and the product is dairy-free with no cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the low fat content (0.72 g/100 g) and absence of dairy cholesterol are favourable for cardiovascular health, the added sugar (5.83 g/100 g) in this sweetened packaged hazelnut milk contributes to CVD risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should opt for unsweetened hazelnut milk to avoid excess sugar intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hazelnut Milk (Packaged, Sweetened) is a commercially produced, dairy-free, plant-based milk alternative made primarily from water, hazelnuts, and added sugar, sold in shelf-stable or refrigerated packaging. It has a mild, slightly sweet, nutty flavour with a thin, watery texture compared to dairy milk, as commercial hazelnut milks typically contain less than 2% hazelnut content. Per 100 g, it provides 45 kcal, 0.41 g protein, 0.72 g fat, 9.45 g carbohydrate, 5.83 g sugar, 0.41 g dietary fibre, and 49 mg sodium. Calcium and iron are reported as 0 mg in the HPB Singapore database, indicating this product is not fortified. The product is vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it suitable for lactose-intolerant individuals and those following plant-based diets. However, the added sugar content (5.83 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage, which is a concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. Unsweetened variants are preferred for clinical populations. The glycemic index is estimated at 39 (low) based on analogous nut-based plant milks from the University of Sydney GI Database, with a glycemic load of approximately 8.8 per 240 ml serving.
common_user_queries: Is hazelnut milk good for diabetes?
Hazelnut milk vs almond milk nutrition
Can I drink hazelnut milk if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F01020008) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and is clearly documented as such.
notes: Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this packaged hazelnut milk product are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F01020008) as of April 2026. Calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source, indicating this product is not fortified with calcium or iron; these are confirmed zero values per the HPB database. No zero-macro justification is required as protein_g (0.41), fat_g (0.72), and carbohydrate_g (9.45) are all non-zero. GI is an analogous estimate at 39 (low) based on nut-based plant milks from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct hazelnut milk GI measurement was available as of April 2026. This product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage due to added sugar content of 5.83 g/100 g, which triggers SSB hard gates for diabetes and hypertension health suitability assessments. Multilingual local names are not applicable for this globally distributed packaged product with no specific Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cultural identity; the cuisine is classified as Global/Western.
